hits.name: Zuccardi 2025 Serie A Torrontés (Argentina)
hits.review: Jasmine aromas fill the nose with attractive floral notes that echo on the palate. Subtle herbal flavors meet with grapefruit and candied lemon peel, adding brightness and citrus character. Good acidity provides balance, making this an aromatic, refreshing wine.

region.name: Argentina
region.description: Over the past decade Argentina has evolved from a country not well known on the global wine scene to the New World’s fastest growing exporter of wines. The leading grape in Argentina in terms of reputation and quantity is Malbec, a Bordeaux variety imported to Argentina from France in the mid 19th century. Other red varieties produced in Argentina include Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Bonarda (known as Charbono in its native Italy), Syrah, Tempranillo and Pinot Noir. Among white wines, the Argentinian wine region’s signature grape is Torrontés, which yields a floral, tropical-tasting wine. The country also produces Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Grigio.There are seven denominated Argentinian wine regions: Mendoza, Salta, Neuquén, Río Negro, Catamarca, La Rioja and San Juan. Among these, Mendoza is the leader in terms of quantity and quality, with roughly 80% of the nation’s wine production centered in this desert region located almost directly across the Andes Mountains from Santiago, Chile, and about 650 miles due west of Buenos Aires. Neuquén and Río Negro, meanwhile, are in southerly Patagonia, while Salta is in the north of the country, near the border with Bolivia (Torrontés is a specialty in Salta); Catamarca, La Rioja and San Juan are lesser known regions that mostly satisfy the domestic market.Wine Enthusiast’s Buying Guide features thousands of Argentine wine ratings from all over the country. Conduct a quick and easy search to find ratings from your favorite producers to access the Argentinian wine guide.

hits.name: vigneti zabu 2025 Sensuale Grillo Grillo (Sicilia)
hits.review: The nose is a mix of crushed wild herbs, salted nuts, fresh lemon and green and yellow apples. Those tart and sweet apple notes are joined by star fruit to headline the palate, while nuance comes from a slice of very fresh, juicy but not overripe pineapple and more salted nuts, plus piercing acidity.

hits.name: vigneti zabu 2025 Grillo Grillo (Sicilia)
hits.review: The nose opens with green apple, limeade, white peach and jasmine, then turns grassy and earthy. The palate has a similar feel, with tart apple and ripe melon flavors creating a juicy, full feel, but that grassiness lingering as an undertone. A fiercely tart, salty current rips down the middle to keep you on your toes.

hits.name: Tussock Jumper 2025 Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: This is quite reductive at first sniff, those onion and flint aromas hovering above the lime and tropical fruit notes. The palate offers crisp acidity with some pithy texture and a hint of residual sugar sticking out, giving it young, slightly unresolved feel.

hits.name: Trivento 2025 Reserve White Malbec (Mendoza)
hits.review: This white Malbec is light and crisp, with a hint of apple on the nose. The palate shows touches of herbs and citrus fruit. An easy-drinking wine that is perfect for a sunny day.

region.name: Argentina
region.description: Over the past decade Argentina has evolved from a country not well known on the global wine scene to the New World’s fastest growing exporter of wines. The leading grape in Argentina in terms of reputation and quantity is Malbec, a Bordeaux variety imported to Argentina from France in the mid 19th century. Conduct a quick and easy search to find ratings from your favorite producers to access the Argentinian wine guide.

hits.name: Sokol Blosser 2025 Estate Rosé Pinot Noir (Dundee Hills)
hits.review: Sokol Blosser's rosé never disappoints. The latest vintage is a fresh and airy pink version of Pinot Noir that makes its introductions with peach, strawberry, filbert and toasted marshmallow aromas. Its acidity is gentler than recent vintages, with flavors of raspberry, grapefruit and a touch of cinnamon. Feel free to enjoy your summer with bottles of this wine in your refrigerator.

region.name: Willamette Valley
region.description: The Willamette Valley Wine Region is one of the most influential wine producing regions in all of Oregon as well as the entire Pacific Northwest. The region spans from the Columbia River in the north all the way down to Eugene in the South. It is by far Oregon’s largest AVA and is dominated by plantings of Pinot Noir and smaller amounts of Chardonnay, Pinot Gris and Riesling. The Willamette Valley is subdivided into six smaller appellations: Chehalem Mountains AVA, Dundee Hills AVA, McMinnville AVA, Ribbon Ridge AVA, Yamhill-Carlton District AVA and Eola-Amity Hills AVA. According to our Willamette Valley Wine Guide the mild, cool and somewhat moist climate provides ideal conditions for Pinot Noir, creating wines that are often considered the bridge between California and Burgundy style. The Pinot Noirs from this area tend to have black cherry and Dr. Pepper aromas and flavors with nuances of earth and herbs. For a great pairing, try these wines with herb-crusted roasted chicken, pork loin and mild game birds. Established in 1984, this burgeoning region now has more than 200 wineries with many of them producing the top selections in our Willamette Valley Wine Ratings. Check out our Williamette Valley Wine Reviews for more information on these standout wines.

countries_menu.lvl2: United States > Willamette Valley > Dundee Hills

name.value: Sokol Blosser 2025 Estate Rosé Pinot Noir (Dundee Hills)

hits.name: Sileni 2025 Cellar Selection Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: A pungent pop of peach, pineapple, guava and bell pepper opens. The shill acidity makes the tongue tingle and the mouth pucker, but it's all set up to suggest this may just need several more months to a year in bottle to knit together.

hits.name: Seaside Cellars 2025 Sauvignon Blanc (New Zealand)
hits.review: This throws a ferociously aromatic punch of feline-like pungency, the green bell pepper, passionfruit and blackcurrant leaf notes following just behind. The palate is more tempered, with mouth-watering acidity rushing across the tongue and highlighting fruit rather than pungency.

hits.name: sarl select vins 2025 Ambrine Grenache (Corbières)
hits.other_info: VINIFICATION : TASTING : MAIN GRAPE VARIETIES : Harvested at optimum maturity, the grapes are destemmed and sorted before vatting. Gentle extraction i
hits.review: This is a more rustic style, starting with aromas of marjoram, black plum and charred meat on the nose. The palate is quite herbal with thyme, sage and wild mint flavors against a tart red fruit.

region.name: Languedoc-Roussillon
region.description: In the south, the hot, sun-drenched vineyards of the Languedoc and Roussillon wine regions don’t just produce loads of inexpensive wine under the Pays d’Oc umbrella. Some AOCs, such as Corbières, Minervois, Coteaux du Languedoc and Côtes de Roussillon offer a magic mix of great value, history and fascinating herbal (garrigue) and fruity flavors.A new hierarchy has recently been agreed upon for Languedoc appellations, with AOC Languedoc wines at the bottom of the pyramid, followed by Grands Vins de Languedoc (such as Saint-Chinian and Faugères) and finally the Grand Crus du Languedoc (such as Pic Saint Loup and La Clape).A veritable alphabet soup of grape varieties are grown, with the most common being Syrah, Carignan, Mourvèdre, Grenache and Cinsault. Search our Languedoc-Roussillon wine guide’s hundreds of Languedoc-Roussillon wine reviews for more details on individual wines and for our comprehensive database of Languedoc-Roussillon wine ratings.

hits.name: sarl select vins 2025 Ambrine Rhône-Style White Blend (Languedoc)
hits.other_info: Direct pressing with juice selection and cold storageIn concrete vats on fine lees.
hits.review: Hints of banana flower and coconut are edged by a sandstone minerality on the nose of this bottling. The palate sizzles with acidity and holds a firm texture through the light coconut and guava flavors.

region.name: Languedoc-Roussillon
region.description: In the south, the hot, sun-drenched vineyards of the Languedoc and Roussillon wine regions don’t just produce loads of inexpensive wine under the Pays d’Oc umbrella. hits.name: sarl select vins 2025 Ambrine Shiraz-Syrah (La Clape)
hits.other_info: Harvested at optimum maturity. Traditional vinification in concrete vats with punching down and pumping over. Long vatting Aged 12 months in concrete
hits.review: Intriguing lines of toasted fennel, dark and rustic plum fruit, hints of lavender and rish spicecake introduce the nose to this bottling. The palate's licorice and garrigue flavors decorate a charred fruit core, with flashes of rosemary on the finish.

name.value: sarl select vins 2025 Ambrine Shiraz-Syrah (La Clape)

hits.name: sarl select vins 2025 Ambrine Bourboulenc (La Clape)
hits.other_info: Direct pressing with juice selection and cold stalling.3 to 4 months on fine lees in concrete vats.
hits.review: Tightly woven aromas of jasmine, plumeria and guava are cut by a stony, steel minerality on the nose of this bottling. The palate is lightly bodied and somewhat delicate with honeydew flavors.

name.value: sarl select vins 2025 Ambrine Bourboulenc (La Clape)

hits.name: Saracco 2025 Moscato (Moscato d'Asti)
hits.label: w_1472239.png
hits.review: White peaches kissed with fresh mint flowing through jasmine petals and wild thyme, each aromatic layer floating like summer memories. The palate dances with perfect balance, where ripe nectarines and elegant sweetness ride waves of vibrant acidity. This is Moscato d'Asti showing its truest self, where delicate bubbles and pristine fruit linger. Pour it with spicy Vietnamese spring rolls now, save some for fresh peach tart later, this wine knows no boundaries.

region.name: Piedmont
region.description: Located in North West Italy, the Piedmont Wine Region has the largest area in terms of DOC and DOCG wines. Meaning “foot of the mountain,” due to its proximity to the Alps, Piedmont wines including Barolo, Barberesco, Gattinara, Ghemme and Gavi all benefit from the slopes and terrain characteristic of the region. Nebbiolo, the dominant grape variety for reds, produces wines with tannic and acidic qualities that can also have fairly high alcohol content. This combination makes the distinguished and cellar-worthy wines of Barolo and Barberesco some of the most sought-after, according to our Piedmont Wine Guide. In terms of value, the Langhe, Ghemme and Gattinara areas also produce wine with the Nebbiolo grape, but sell at a fraction of the price. Barbera and Dolcetto offer buoyant red wines that are best enjoyed in their youth. The crisp and fresh white wines from the Gavi DOC are made from the Cortese grape and are suitable pairings for lighter fare, such as seafood or poultry. Moscato is also grown in Piedmont and is responsible for most of the sparkling and sweet dessert-style wines produced in the region. For an extensive list of red and white wines, read our detailed Piedmont Wine Ratings.

hits.name: Santa Julia 2025 La Oveja Torrontés (Mendoza)
hits.review: Sweet pears and golden apple aromas introduce this wine with inviting fruit character. The palate delivers savory melon and apple flavors supported by moderate freshness that keeps things balanced and lively. An enjoyable Torrontés—perfect to sip on its own during hot summer days.

region.name: Argentina
region.description: Over the past decade Argentina has evolved from a country not well known on the global wine scene to the New World’s fastest growing exporter of wines. The leading grape in Argentina in terms of reputation and quantity is Malbec, a Bordeaux variety imported to Argentina from France in the mid 19th century. Other red varieties produced in Argentina include Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Bonarda (known as Charbono in its native Italy), Syrah, Tempranillo and Pinot Noir. Among white wines, the Argentinian wine region’s signature grape is Torrontés, which yields a floral, tropical-tasting wine. The country also produces Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Grigio.There are seven denominated Argentinian wine regions: Mendoza, Salta, Neuquén, Río Negro, Catamarca, La Rioja and San Juan. Among these, Mendoza is the leader in terms of quantity and quality, with roughly 80% of the nation’s wine production centered in this desert region located almost directly across the Andes Mountains from Santiago, Chile, and about 650 miles due west of Buenos Aires. Neuquén and Río Negro, meanwhile, are in southerly Patagonia, while Salta is in the north of the country, near the border with Bolivia (Torrontés is a specialty in Salta); Catamarca, La Rioja and San Juan are lesser known regions that mostly satisfy the domestic market.Wine Enthusiast’s Buying Guide features thousands of Argentine wine ratings from all over the country. Conduct a quick and easy search to find ratings from your favorite producers to access the Argentinian wine guide.

hits.name: Pfitscher 2025 Cortazo Riserva Red (Alto Adige)
hits.review: Purple fruits combine with baking spices, while subtle but persistent earthy undertones carry through from the nose to the finish. Tart red fruits add vibrancy, shifting the wine into a fresh gear, lifted by lively acidity. Each glass brings the sensation of a cool mountain breeze, making this wine as refreshing as it is complex.

region.name: Northeastern Italy
region.description: The northeast corner of Italy, surrounded by the Austrian, Slovenian, and Swiss borders, is home to three major wine producing regions: the Veneto, Friuli-Venezia Giulia and Trentino-Alto Adige. In the Veneto, the majority of the reds consist of Corvina, Rondinella and Molinara. These versatile grapes help to produce the light and fruity early-consumption wines of Valpolicella and Bardolino. However when the grapes are dried using the passito method, they are used to produce the massive and robust wines of Amarone della Valpolicella, which consistently earn some of the highest ratings in our Northeast Italian Wine Reviews. The slopes and climate of Trentino-Alto Adige allow Pinot Grigio to thrive, but also have inspired the plantings of some alternate white varietals such as Riesling, Gewürztraminer, Sauvignon Blanc and Pinot Bianco. The whites of Friuli-Venezia Giulia, especially the varietal wines from the Colli Orientali DOC, fare extremely well according to our Northeastern Italy Wine Ratings. Those created from Tocai Friulano and Pinot Grigio are some of the region’s best and offer fresh acidity with an abundance of orchard fruit flavors. To learn more about the wines of the region, reference our Northeastern Italy Buying Guide and our Italian white wine reviews.

hits.name: Panther Ridge 2025 Rosé of Pinot Noir (Petaluma Gap)
hits.other_info: Picked at 21.4 Brix, fermented in stainless steel, TA 6.24, pH 3.27
hits.review: Aromas of fresh melon, lemon, and flowers swirl on the nose of this stainless steel fermented rosé of Pinot Noir, giving way to flavors of dusty berry, pithy citrus, and ginger on the palate before a broad and lushly textured finish.

region.name: Sonoma
region.description: The wine region of Sonoma is home to some of the most historic vineyards and wineries in California. In the 1850s, Hungarian Agoston Haraszathy planted various grapes in Sonoma that he was confident could rival European varieties, and today it is a leading wine region in California as well as the U.S. The Pinot Noir and Chardonnay from the Russian River Valley and Sonoma Coast are predominantly made by smaller wineries and have experienced increased popularity, as can be explored in our Sonoma Wine Guide. Dry Creek Valley Zinfandel, the only varietal that the U.S. can call its own because it was the first planted in California, is rich with ripe and jammy berry characteristics and can gain complexity with oak barrel aging. The same goes for the elegant Cabernet Sauvignon wines of the Alexander Valley, which tend to be notably food-friendly. To see which wines stand out in Sonoma, check out the ratings in our Sonoma Wine Reviews.

name.value: Panther Ridge 2025 Rosé of Pinot Noir (Petaluma Gap)

hits.name: Oyster Bay 2025 Pinot Grigio (Hawkes Bay)
hits.review: Slightly confected green apple and white peach aromas lead to a crisp, fruity palate that's straightforward and a little synthetic but refreshing.

hits.name: Oyster Bay 2025 Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: This is a young, shrill and somewhat disjointed Sauvignon that feels a little volatile, with an acetone tinge to the citrus and passionfruit notes, and a burn of sulphur on the nostrils.

hits.name: Overstone 2025 Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: Whizz, bang, pop aromas of pink grapefruit, lemon-lime, passionfruit and bell pepper take your senses for a heady ride. A ping of bright acidity and fruity tang round out the refreshment.

hits.name: Moroki 2025 Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: Some trapped CO2 lingers in glass upon first pour but dissipates with time in glass. It's fresh and fruity with lime and passionfruit, a hint of sweetness and a short finish.

hits.name: Weekday by Provenance Vineyards 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (California)
hits.review: The tagline says to sip without sacrifice; this is a campaign we can get behind. This is one of the many wine offerings with zero sugar, low calories, and Low ABV that tastes like wine. Supple, lively aromas of white peach, citrus, savory herbs, and a dash of acidity keep this selection vibrant and continue onto the palate.

name.value: Weekday by Provenance Vineyards 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (California)

hits.name: Weekday by Provenance Vineyards 2024 Chardonnay (California)
hits.review: You know the wine game has changed when a legacy brand produces selections with zero sugar, low calories, and Low ABV that taste like wine. Provenance Vineyard has Weekday, and it is made from organic grapes, which gives full flavor without compromising. Rich aromas of pear, yellow apple, and honeysuckle wrapped around mouth-watering citrus and orchard fruits to finish. Pair with pasta and cream sauces

hits.name: Weekday by Provenance Vineyards 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon (California)
hits.review: This wine is the newest offering of low-ABV wines by an industry leader, Provenance Vineyard. The aromas on the nose are pretty ripe and supple for wine with no sugar added: dark plums, blackberry, Black Olive, and mineral on the nose. The flavors on the palate are bright, raspberry, rhubarb, a slight zest of citrus, and a dry mineral finish.

name.value: Weekday by Provenance Vineyards 2024 Cabernet Sauvignon (California)

hits.name: Waterbrook 2024 Reserve Chardonnay (Columbia Valley)
hits.review: This is one creamy, buttery Chardonnay that starts off with aromas of lemon curd and fresh-shredded coconut. An expansive mouthfeel accompanies the wine's big, rich flavors of lime sorbet, coconut, butter and an apple baked with cinnamon spikes. More acidity would have been welcomed with open arms.

region.name: Columbia Valley
region.description: Spanning across the central and southern areas of Washington State lies the Columbia Valley Wine Region. It is the state’s largest AVA, covering nearly one-third of the state’s landmass. The wines of the region have been described as having the potential to combine the structure and finesse of the Old world wines with the approachable fruit and ripeness of the New World style. Our Columbia Valley Wine Ratings illustrate the improvement in quality of these wines over the last decade or so. The main white grape varietals grown in the region are Chardonnay and Riesling which are discernible by their vibrant fruit and crisp acidity. The red varietals are predominantly Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot, with a recent increase in Syrah particularly in the Columbia Valley sub region Walla Walla. It was the Merlot craze of the 1990s that sparked the growth of the Columbia Valley; however, it is the lush texture and concentrated berry fruit of all the red wines that have sustained its success according to our Columbia Valley Wine Reviews.

hits.name: Waterbrook 2024 Chardonnay (Columbia Valley)
hits.review: Apples, apples and more apples is the theme of the day for this Chardonnay. The wine's apple aroma is complemented by notes of talc and citrus. The apple flavor, think Golden Delicious, is joined by a healthy dose of figs. This is a lighter-bodied wine, with a smooth mouthfeel and restrained acidity.

region.name: Columbia Valley

It is the state’s largest AVA, covering nearly one-third of the state’s landmass. hits.name: Waterbrook 2024 Sangiovese Rosé (Columbia Valley)
hits.review: This is no shy and retiring rosé. It strikes first with tart red cherry, peach and wet slate aromas that fill the local airspace. Then it smacks you upside the head with acidity furious enough to make the hairs on your arm stand on end. The wine feels tart in the mouth, with lemon, basil, tonic water and Granny Smith apple flavors lighting up the room.

hits.name: Waterbrook 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (Columbia Valley)
hits.review: The way a tart lemon pith aroma interacts with the sweetness of a fresh-cut grass lawn and a touch of fried sage is quite an attraction introduction to this Sauvignon Blanc. Its aggressive acidity blazes a path for the wine's green tea served with a wedge of lemon flavor. This is quite the tangy treat.

hits.name: Waldschütz 2024 Strass Grüner Veltliner (Kamptal)
hits.review: This shows crisp apple and zesty lime flavors. The palate shows nice texture and balance, with subtle hints of baking spice on the finish. Fresh, approachable, and precise.

hits.name: Waldschütz 2024 Venesse Riesling (Kamptal)
hits.review: Linear and elegant, with crisp apple and zesty lime flavors. The palate is bright and well-textured, showing balance and subtle energy, with a touch of baking spice on the finish. Fresh and approachable, offering a precise and refined expression.

hits.name: Wairau River 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: This comes with all the bombastic pungency you'd expect from this famed style. Is there peach pit, grape and passionfruit? You bet. Green bell pepper and snap pea? Sure thing. That “eau de feline” essence? Why of course. Zippy, fresh and vegetal right to the finish.

hits.name: Waipara Springs 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (Waipara Valley)
hits.review: A pleasant pop of peach pit, guava, grass and snow pea aromas lead to a palate that's (mostly) dry and pithy with crisp acidity and a limey tang to end.

hits.name: Wai Wai 2024 Sauvignon Blanc (Marlborough)
hits.review: All tropical and passionfruit with a green herbal pungency, this shows a touch of residual sugar on the palate but, especially if well chilled, is a refreshing drop nonetheless.
